answer: DynamoDB typically offers single-digit millisecond latency, but high latency can occur due to inefficient queries or suboptimal configurations. Use CloudWatch metrics and DAX for performance improvements. Request hedging can also help meet very low latency requirements.

results.title: Troubleshooting latency issues in Amazon DynamoDB - Amazon DynamoDB
results.snippet: # Troubleshooting latency issues in Amazon DynamoDB. If your workload appears to experience high latency, you can analyze the CloudWatch `SuccessfulRequestLatency` metric, and check the average latency and median latency through percentile metrics (p50) to see if it’s related to DynamoDB. Some variation in latency metrics, particularly in higher percentiles, is expected and can be seen as a result of DynamoDB-driven background operations that help maintain high availability and durability for your data stored in DynamoDB tables or transient infrastructure issues. Requests over an already initialized connection deliver DynamoDB's consistent low latency. * **Implement request hedging:** For very low p99 latency requirements, consider implementing request hedging. For a helpful discussion on this topic, see Tuning AWS Java SDK HTTP request settings for latency-aware Amazon DynamoDB applications. + DynamoDB Accelerator (DAX): A fully managed, highly available, in-memory cache for DynamoDB that delivers up to a 10x performance improvement, from milliseconds to microseconds, even at millions of requests per second.
